description: URL or access point for the raw data.

Contributor roles using the CRediT (Contributor Roles Taxonomy) for the principal investigator or creator team. Specifies the specific contributions made to this dataset (e.g., Conceptualization, Data Curation, Methodology). Note: roles are specified here rather than on Person directly, since the same person may have different roles across different datasets.
End date of data collection
URL or access point for the erratum.
List of identifier types removed during de-identification.
Category of limitation (e.g., scope, coverage, temporal, methodological).
Code(s) used to represent missing values for this variable. Examples: "NA", "-999", "null", "". Multiple codes may be specified.
The precision or number of decimal places for numeric variables.
Explanation of how representativeness was validated or verified.
Start date of data collection
The dataset that this relationship points to. Can be specified by identifier, URL, or Dataset object.
Known accuracy or performance metrics for the automated tools (if available). Include metric name and value (e.g., "spaCy F1: 0.95", "GPT-4 Accuracy: 92%").

List of automated annotation tools with their versions. Format each entry as "ToolName version" (e.g., "spaCy 3.5.0", "NLTK 3.8", "GPT-4 turbo"). Use "unknown" for version if not available (e.g., "Custom NER Model unknown").
Whether the data was inferred or derived from other data
Whether the data was validated or verified in any way
Specific subsets or features of the dataset affected by this bias.
Type of agreement metric used (Cohen's kappa, Fleiss' kappa, Krippendorff's alpha, percentage agreement, etc.).
Number of annotations collected per data item. Multiple annotations per item enable calculation of inter-annotator agreement.
Demographic information about annotators, if available and relevant (e.g., geographic location, language background, expertise level).
Indication whether official archival versions of external resources are included.
For research involving minors, what assent procedures were used? How was developmentally appropriate assent obtained?
The type of bias identified, using standardized categories from the Artificial Intelligence Ontology (AIO).
Was informed consent obtained from all participants?
What type of consent was obtained (e.g., written, verbal, electronic, implied through participation)?
URL for contribution guidelines or process.
Can this dataset be linked to other datasets in ways that might compromise participant privacy?
Systematic patterns in annotator disagreements (e.g., by demographic group, annotation difficulty, task type).
List of examples of known/previous uses of the dataset.
For participants unable to provide their own consent, how was guardian or surrogate consent obtained?
Indicates compliance with the Health Insurance Portability and Accountability Act (HIPAA). HIPAA applies to protected health information in the United States.
Information about whether dataset involves human subjects research, including IRB approval, ethics review, and regulatory compliance.
Indicates whether data subjects can be identified.
Measured agreement between annotators (e.g., Cohen's kappa value, Fleiss' kappa, Krippendorff's alpha).
Does this dataset involve human subjects research?
Was Institutional Review Board (IRB) approval obtained? Include approval number and institution if applicable.
Is this subset a split of the larger dataset, e.g., is it a set for model training, testing, or validation?
Whether collection was direct from individuals
Indicates whether the sample is random.
Indicates whether the sample is representative of the larger set.
Indicates whether it is a sample of a larger set.
Is this subset a subpopulation of the larger dataset, e.g., is it a set of data for a specific demographic?
Is there a label or target associated with each instance?
DOI or URL of the latest dataset version.
Automated annotation tools used in dataset creation.
Method used for de-identification (e.g., HIPAA Safe Harbor).
Known or suspected causes of missing data (e.g., sensor failures, participant dropout, privacy constraints).
References to one or more MissingInfo objects describing missing data.
Other regulatory compliance frameworks applicable to this dataset (e.g., CCPA, PIPEDA, industry-specific regulations).
Privacy protections and anonymization procedures for human research participants, including reidentification risk assessment.
What privacy-preserving techniques were applied (e.g., differential privacy, k-anonymity, data masking)?
Format of the raw data before any preprocessing.
Recommended approaches for users to address this limitation.
What regulatory frameworks govern this human subjects research (e.g., 45 CFR 46, HIPAA)?
What is the assessed risk of re-identification? What measures were taken to minimize this risk?
Related datasets with typed relationships (e.g., supplements, derives from, is version of). Use DatasetRelationship class to specify relationship types.
The type of relationship (e.g., derives_from, supplements, is_version_of). Uses DatasetRelationshipTypeEnum for standardized relationship types.
URL to a repository of known dataset uses.
Role of the data collector (e.g., researcher, crowdworker)
How this limitation affects the scope or applicability of the dataset.
Type of raw source (sensor, database, user input, web scraping, etc.).
Does the research involve any special populations that require additional protections (e.g., minors, pregnant women, prisoners)?
Indicates whether any subpopulations are explicitly identified.
Category of intended use (e.g., research, clinical, educational, commercial, policy).
What software was used as part of this dataset property?
